answer: Bachelor of Science (Honours) in Intelligent Robotics is offered by Faculty of Artificial Intelligence and Engineering (FAIE). The programme page lists registration (R/0788/6/00177) 01/31 and accreditation (MQA/SWA14238). The Bachelor of Science (Honours) Intelligent Robotics is a 3-year programme that strikes an exquisite balance between the fundamentals of engineering and hands-on practical skills. This multidisciplinary programme combines electronics, robotics, artificial intelligence, automation, and computer programming. It adopts a modern learning approach with early exposure to real-world applications. Graduates will be agile knowledge workers in the IR4.0 age and beyond, highly sought after by the industry.

answer: March/April BYOC elective subjects include: Fundamentals of Marketing, Digital Transformation Strategy, Personal Finance, Radio Network Planning Towards 5G, Media Anthropology, Project Management, Motion Capture, Media Law, Corporate Strategy, Social Media Strategies, Introductory Mobile Application Development, Basic Filmmaking, Fundamental of Wireless Communications. A practical way to choose: 1. Pick Project Management or Corporate Strategy if you want stronger project leadership. 2. Pick Introductory Mobile Application Development if you want to connect robotics with apps. 3. Pick Radio Network Planning Towards 5G or Fundamental of Wireless Communications if you like connected robots and networks. 4. Pick Motion Capture or Basic Filmmaking if you want creative or interactive robotics work. 5. Pick Personal Finance if you want a useful life skill outside the technical track.

Pick Principal of Finance or Fundamental of Marketing if you want broader business awareness.

Subject Code: ARM6113

Title: Technical Calculus

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 1

Trimester: 1

- Determine the probability, mean and variance for various distributions in discrete and continuous random variables.

- Fourier series
- Complex number functions and vector algebra
- Probability

- Compute the limits, derivatives, integrals and extrema of single-variable functions.

- Construct the power series and the Fourier series representations of functions.
- Determine the probability, mean and variance for various distributions in discrete and continuous random variables.

Subject Code: ARL6113

Title: Electrical Circuits

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 1

Trimester: 1

Intake Cycle Label: March/April 2026

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 46 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Pre-Requisite: None shown in structure

Objective:
To provide a basic understanding of the laws of circuit theory and the behaviour of circuits during steady-state and transient conditions.

Assessment:
- Test/Quiz: 20%
- Assignment: 20%
- Lab: 10%
- Final Examination: 50%

Laboratory:
- CK1: Thevenin’s Theorem and Superposition Theorem
- CK2: AC Circuits

Course Contents:
- Circuit elements and signal waveforms
- Basic laws of electric circuit
- Network analysis
- Sinusoidal steady state analysis
- Transient analysis

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Explain the basic concepts and laws of electric circuits, and different types of signals.
- Solve the electrical networks using nodal and mesh analysis techniques.
- Compute the impedance, resonance and complex power of sinusoidal circuits.
- Analyse the transient response of RL, RC and RLC circuits.
- Construct a circuit by using circuit theory experiment board to verify the law of electric circuit and phasor relationship.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

- Apply the techniques used to process text files in computer programs.
- Design complete and properly structured programs.

Subject Code: ARE6123

Title: Digital Design

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 1

Trimester: 3

Intake Cycle Label: October/November 2026

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 47 hours (lectures, tutorials & laboratory experiments)

Pre-Requisite: None shown in structure

Objective:
To provide a basic understanding of the basics of logic circuits and their applications in digital system.

Assessment:
- Lab: 10%
- Assignment: 15%
- Test/Quiz: 15%
- Final Examination: 60%

Laboratory:
- DL1: Logic Gates And Their Applications
- DL2: Flip-flop And Their Applications

Course Contents:
- Introduction
- Number systems and codes
- Algebraic methods for analysis and synthesis of logic circuits
- Simplification of switching functions
- Introduction to sequential logic
- Modular combinational logic

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Distinguish the analog and digital systems, apply positional notations, number systems and computer codes in digital systems.
- Use Boolean algebra, the Karnaugh maps and Quine-McCluskey tabular methods to simplify and design logic circuits.
- Apply the concepts of sequential logic and memory devices in digital systems.
- Design modular combinational circuits using encoders, decoders, multiplexers and demultiplexers.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: Subject Code all.docx

To acquire technical knowledge in the field of actuators and sensors, and to design programmable logic controllers used in various industrial applications.

Assessment:
- Test: 10%
- Assignment: 30%

- Final exam: 40%

- Actuators
- Sensors
- Gears
- Controllers

- Describe the operation of actuators in industrial applications.
- Demonstrate the usage of sensors in industrial applications.
- Construct programmable logic controllers with sensor and actuator using practical software tools.

- Measure signals in the frequency domain using spectrum analyzers/signal analyzers.

- Interfacing examples

- Explain the fundamental features and operations of contemporary microcontrollers and microprocessors.
- Explain the hardware architecture of a typical 8051 microcontroller.
- Analyse the MCS-51 Instruction Set.
- Develop assembly language programs for 8051 based embedded systems.

- Test: 15%

- Circuit analysis using Laplace transform

- Network synthesis of passive elements
- Passive filters

- Perform signal classification, signal modelling and convolution.

- Compute network parameters for two-port networks.
- Solve for the time and frequency behaviour of electric circuits using Laplace Transform and state variable analysis.
- Construct one-port networks and filters.

Key contents: Introduction to signals; Network topology; Circuit analysis using Laplace transform; State variable analysis of RLC networks; Two-port networks; Network synthesis of passive elements; Passive filters Learning outcomes: Perform signal classification, signal modelling and convolution.; Analyse electric circuits using network graphs.; Compute network parameters for two-port networks.; Solve for the time and frequency behaviour of electric circuits using Laplace Transform and state variable analysis.; Construct one-port networks and filters.

Subject Code: ARM6133

Title: Linear Algebra and Numerical Methods

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 2

Trimester: 2

Intake Cycle Label: July/August 2027

Contact Hours: 48 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Objective:
This course introduces students to the mathematical concepts and analysis methods of linear algebra, numerical methods and optimization for solving practical problems in science and engineering.

Assessment:
- Test 1: 15%
- Test 2: 10%
- Quiz: 5%
- Assignment: 10%
- Final exam: 60%

Course Contents:
- Linear algebra
- Numerical methods
- Optimization

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Use various methods in linear algebra to solve systems of algebraic equations.
- Apply numerical methods to solve mathematical problems including interpolation, differentiation, integration, root finding, ordinary differential equations and systems of linear equations.
- Determine the optimal solutions to the optimization problems using appropriate methods.

Subject Code: ARR6123

Title: Robotics – Machine Design and Mechanisms

Aliases: Robotics - Machine Design and Mechanisms

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 2

Trimester: 2

Intake Cycle Label: July/August 2027

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 44 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Pre-Requisite: None shown in structure

Objective:
To develop the skills for designing linkage mechanisms and mechanical systems that contain the combination of motors, gear trains, linkages and other moving parts.

Assessment:
- Test: 20%
- Assignment: 20%
- Lab Test: 20%
- Final exam: 40%

Course Contents:
- Introduction
- Kinematics fundamentals
- Linkage synthesis
- Engineering materials
- Screws, fasteners and nonpermanent joints
- Bearings, couplings and lubrication
- Flexible mechanical elements

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Describe the fundamental of linkage mechanisms.
- Choose suitable engineering materials based on criteria such as strength, flexibility, machinability, cost and weight.
- Evaluate suitable mechanical systems which contain the combination of motors, gear trains, linkages and other moving parts.
- Build a mechanical system which contain the combination of motors, gear trains, linkages and other moving parts.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

- Analyse properties of control systems using frequency domain methods.
- Design compensators for control systems using strategic thinking.

answer:
Subject Code: ARL6123

Title: Electrical Machines and Power Systems

Aliases: Electrical Machines and Power System

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 2

Trimester: 3

Intake Cycle Label: October/November 2027

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 47 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Pre-Requisite: None shown in structure

Objective:
To introduce the basic concepts and working principles of electrical machines and transformers as well as electrical power systems and their protection methods.

Assessment:
- Test: 10%
- Assignment: 20%
- Lab Assessment: 20%
- Final exam: 50%

Course Contents:
- Introduction to machinery principles
- Three-phase circuits
- Transformers
- D.C. machines
- AC machines (Induction Motors & Synchronous Motors)

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Apply the machinery principles and balanced three-phase power system circuit analysis on electrical machines and transformers.
- Analyze the circuit characteristics and performance of transformers.
- Analyze the circuit characteristics and performance of DC and AC machines.
- Demonstrate the characteristics and performance of single-phase transformer and induction motor.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: Subject Code all.docx

Notes:
- Normalized title to plural 'Systems'.

- Part 1 – Presentation: 10%

- Demonstrate self-awareness in continuous lifelong-learning skills.
- Apply integrated problem solving to propose a research paper/proposal for commercialization of the product.

Pre-Requisite: Completed 60 credit hours (excluding Art & Humanities subjects); Part 2 follows Part 1

Objective:
Continuation of project work through implementation, testing/evaluation, final report, research paper/commercialisation proposal, and presentation.

Assessment:
- Part 2 – General Effort, Project Implementation, Research paper/Commercialisation proposal: 25%
- Part 2 – Presentation: 10%
- Final Report: 30%

Course Contents:
- Project implementation and testing/evaluation
- Final report
- Research paper/commercialisation proposal
- Presentation

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Develop the strategies for background study, analysis, design, implementation, and evaluation based on real-world problem.
- Demonstrate communication skills in writing and presenting the project design and implementation.
- Employ contemporary digital and numeracy tools to critically analyze problems in artificial intelligence related fields.
- Demonstrate self-awareness in continuous lifelong-learning skills.
- Apply integrated problem solving to propose a research paper/proposal for commercialization of the product.

answer:
Subject Code: ARR6143

Title: Robotics – Modelling and Control

Aliases: Robotics - Modelling and Control

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 3

Trimester: 1

Intake Cycle Label: March/April 2028

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 44 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Pre-Requisite: None shown in structure

Objective:
To provides students with rigorous mathematical skills on modelling and control of robotic arm and similar manipulator.

Assessment:
- Test: 20%
- Assignment: 20%
- Lab Test: 20%
- Final exam: 40%

Laboratory:
Control and automation lab, Robotic arms.

Course Contents:
- Representing position and orientation
- Robot modeling
- Forward and inverse kinematics
- Trajectory generation
- Velocity relationships
- Dynamic and control

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Compute the pose of a robotic arm given the configuration, links and angle of each joint.
- Deduce the joint angle of each robotic arm link given the required pose via inverse kinematic.
- Generate the trajectory for the end-effector of a robotic arm via joint-space or Cartesian space schemes using anticipatory thinking.
- Construct linear trajectory-following control scheme for robotic arm.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: Subject Code all.docx

Subject Code: ARC6184

Title: Making Embedded Systems

Category: core

Group: technical

Year: 3

Trimester: 3

Intake Cycle Label: October/November 2028

Credit Hours: 4

Contact Hours: 60 hours (lectures, tutorials and laboratory experiments)

Pre-Requisite: ARC6123 Micro-controllers & Micro-processors

Objective:
To introduce the best practice and the art of putting together an embedded system, from selection of processors, basic hardware requirements, to software design.

Assessment:
- Quiz: 20%
- Test: 20%
- Lab Test: 30%
- Project: 30%

Course Contents:
- Introduction
- Creating a system architecture
- Getting on the hardware
- Inputs, outputs and timers
- Managing the flow of activity
- Communicating with peripherals
- Doing more with less
- PCB design and layout
- PCB fabrication, soldering and reworking

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Describe the hardware and software requirements of a robust embedded systems from datasheet.
- Construct an embedded controller with support circuit from scratch.
- Develop a software framework using systematic thinking for the embedded controller to perform a useful task.
- Design PCB layout for an electronic prototype.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: Subject Code all.docx

Notes:
- Prerequisite overridden by programme structure.

answer:
Subject Code: LDCW6123

Title: Fundamentals of Digital Competence for Programmers

Category: university_course

Group: university

Year: 3

Trimester: 2

Intake Cycle Label: July/August 2028

Credit Hours: 3

Contact Hours: 41 hours

Pre-Requisite: None

Objective:
The course aims to introduce the knowledge and skills of the digital competence and its framework elements. It also exposes learners to the emergence of digital competence as a new approach and skills needed to manage daily life tasks and industry.

Assessment:
- Assignment 1: 30%
- Assignment 2: 30%
- Project: 40%

Course Contents:
- Digital competence and its framework
- Information and data literacy
- Communication and collaboration
- Digital content creation
- Safety in digital world
- Problem solving in digital world
- Distributed version control and Git workflows

Course Learning Outcomes:
- Describe measures for digital safety and solutions for common digital problems in a modern workplace environment.
- Demonstrate ability to work with digital information collaboratively through collaboration and communication tools.
- Build a program collaboratively with digital content.

Source Structure: BSc-IR-March-2026-T2610.pdf

Source Detail: LDCW6123 — Fundamentals of Digital Competence for Programmers.pdf

answer: The Bachelor of Science (Honours) Intelligent Robotics is a 3-year programme that strikes an exquisite balance between the fundamentals of engineering and hands-on practical skills. This multidisciplinary programme combines electronics, robotics, artificial intelligence, automation, and computer programming.
